status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Virtual science laboratory with 3D simulations for physics, chemistry, and biology experiments. Student collaboration, lab reports, and curriculum alignment.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas (students, teachers, admins), and process flows for the virtual science laboratory platform covering physics, chemistry, and biology experiment domains.
acceptance_criteria: At least 5 distinct user personas documented with goals and pain points
Process flows cover all 3 science domains (physics, chemistry, biology)
ROI analysis includes cost comparison vs physical lab infrastructure
Business requirements traceable to product features

plan.description: Research competitor virtual lab platforms (Labster, PhET, Visible Body), curriculum standards (NGSS, AP, IB), and go-to-market positioning for K-12 and higher education segments.
acceptance_criteria: Competitive matrix covering at least 5 competitors with feature comparison
Curriculum standards mapping for NGSS, AP, and IB documented
Positioning statement and differentiation rationale written
Go-to-market strategy with primary and secondary segments defined

plan.description: Conduct user research with students and educators, produce wireframes for the experiment canvas, lab report editor, collaboration workspace, and curriculum browser. Include WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility audit requirements.
acceptance_criteria: User research completed with at least 6 participants (mix of students and teachers)
Wireframes produced for all key screens listed in payload
Color contrast ratios meet WCAG 2.1 AA (4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text)
Keyboard navigation flow documented for all interactive simulation controls
Accessibility audit checklist completed and attached
Accessibility for learners with disabilities
Mobile-responsive for student devices

plan.description: Produce the product requirements document (PRD), prioritized feature roadmap across 3 releases, and success metrics (DAU, experiment completion rate, lab report submission rate, curriculum coverage %).
acceptance_criteria: PRD approved covering all 3 science domains with feature specifications
Roadmap with v1/v2/v3 scope defined and rationale documented
Success metrics with baseline targets and measurement methods specified
Prioritization framework (MoSCoW or RICE) applied to v1 feature set

plan.description: Produce COPPA and FERPA compliance evidence artifacts: data inventory, parental consent flow design, PII handling policy, student record protection controls, and data retention/deletion procedures.

key_controls: no behavioral advertising on under-13 accounts
school official consent model for FERPA

acceptance_criteria: Data inventory identifies all PII fields collected and their legal basis
Parental consent flow compliant with COPPA for users under 13
FERPA school official consent model documented with legitimate educational interest criteria
Data retention policy specifies maximum retention periods per data category
COPPA safe harbor checklist completed with no outstanding gaps

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy (COPPA/FERPA compliant), educator license agreement, and student data processing addendum (DPA) for school district contracts.

required_clauses: no third-party sale of student data

acceptance_criteria: Privacy Policy explicitly addresses COPPA under-13 consent requirements
DPA includes all FERPA-required provisions for school official relationship
Terms of Service distinguishes educator and student account types
All documents reviewed for compliance with US state student privacy laws (NY, CA minimum)
FERPA/COPPA compliance if minors involved

plan.description: Design the database schema for users (students, teachers, admins), experiments, simulation sessions, lab reports, curriculum mappings, collaboration sessions, and audit logs.

key_constraints: student PII isolated in separate schema

acceptance_criteria: ERD diagram produced covering all entities and relationships
All Alembic migration files run cleanly from zero to current schema
Student PII fields encrypted at rest (AES-256)
Soft-delete implemented on users and lab_reports tables
Indexes defined for common query patterns (user experiments, class assignments, curriculum search)

plan.description: Design OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all backend endpoints: auth, user management, experiment catalog, simulation sessions, lab reports, collaboration, curriculum alignment, and admin APIs.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec file validates without errors
All endpoints have request/response schemas with example payloads
Authentication and authorization requirements documented per endpoint
WebSocket protocol for real-time collaboration documented
Error response shapes standardized (RFC 7807 Problem Details)

plan.description: Perform threat model (STRIDE) for the virtual lab platform, covering student data exposure, simulation session hijacking, unauthorized lab report access, and multi-tenant data leakage. Define security controls and penetration test scope.

controls: input sanitization on lab report content
payload.pen_test_scope: API endpoints, WebSocket protocol, authentication flows

acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model document produced covering all 5 asset categories
Risk matrix with likelihood and impact ratings for each identified threat
Security controls mapped to each threat
Penetration test scope document signed off
OWASP Top 10 checklist completed with mitigations documented

plan.description: Set up the SAGE solution configuration for virtual_lab: project.yaml (domain manifest, active modules, theme), prompts.yaml (agent roles: lab_tutor, curriculum_mapper, collaboration_monitor), tasks.yaml (experiment task types). Configure multi-agent coordinator pattern for adaptive lab assistance.

payload.pattern_rationale: Central coordinator decomposes experiment session requests to specialist agents: physics_sim_agent, chemistry_sim_agent, biology_sim_agent, lab_report_reviewer, curriculum_aligner

acceptance_criteria: project.yaml validates and loads without errors via GET /config/yaml/project
prompts.yaml defines system prompts for all 4 agent roles
tasks.yaml registers experiment_guidance, lab_report_review, curriculum_alignment task types
Multi-agent coordinator pattern components (coordinator, specialist agents, task router) documented in CONFIG
make run PROJECT=virtual_lab starts backend cleanly

plan.description: Implement core FastAPI backend: auth service (JWT + refresh), user and organization management (school districts, classes), role-based access control middleware, and COPPA-compliant age verification with parental consent workflow.

payload.coppa_flow: age gate → parent email collection → consent token → account activation

acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/register creates accounts with proper role assignment
JWT access tokens expire in 15 minutes; refresh tokens rotate on use
RBAC middleware rejects unauthorized role access with 403
COPPA consent flow blocks under-13 account activation without parent approval
All password fields hashed with bcrypt (cost factor ≥ 12)
Unit tests cover auth, RBAC, and COPPA consent paths

plan.description: Implement experiment catalog service, simulation session management (launch, progress tracking, state persistence), and the multi-agent coordinator that routes experiment guidance requests to domain specialist agents (physics, chemistry, biology).

payload.coordinator_pattern: routes incoming student help requests → detects domain (physics/chemistry/biology) → dispatches to specialist agent → streams guidance back via SSE

acceptance_criteria: GET /experiments returns paginated catalog with domain filtering
POST /sessions launches a session and returns simulation config payload
Session state persists to Redis within 500ms of each student interaction
Agent coordinator correctly routes to domain specialist based on experiment type
SSE stream delivers guidance tokens within 2s of student query submission
Completed session progress written to PostgreSQL for lab report pre-population

plan.description: Implement lab report service (CRUD, submission, teacher feedback), curriculum alignment engine (maps experiments to NGSS/AP/IB standards), and teacher assignment/progress tracking dashboard API.

payload.curriculum_alignment: keyword + embedding similarity match between experiment metadata and standard descriptors

acceptance_criteria: POST /lab-reports auto-pre-populates from simulation session data
Lab report rich text (TipTap JSON) saved and retrieved without data loss
GET /curriculum/standards returns NGSS, AP, and IB standards searchable by keyword
Curriculum alignment returns top-3 matching standards per experiment with confidence score
Teacher can assign experiments to a class with due date via POST /teacher/assignments
GET /teacher/progress returns per-student completion and submission status

plan.description: Implement real-time student collaboration service using WebSockets (FastAPI WebSocket + Redis Pub/Sub): shared experiment state sync, collaborative lab report co-editing (CRDT-based), and presence/cursor tracking.

payload.state_sync: Redis Pub/Sub for experiment parameter changes broadcast to room participants
payload.co_editing: Yjs CRDT for lab report collaborative editing (server-side Y.js via websocket relay)
payload.presence: cursor position, active section, online status per participant

acceptance_criteria: Two students in same collaboration room see experiment parameter changes within 200ms
Lab report co-editing resolves concurrent edits without data loss (CRDT merge)
Participant presence (online/offline/cursor) updates within 500ms
Room disconnection gracefully handled — student rejoins and receives current state
Teacher can broadcast a message to all students in a room
WebSocket connections authenticated via JWT token in handshake

plan.description: Bootstrap the React 18 + TypeScript frontend with Vite, set up routing (React Router v6), global state (Zustand), API client layer, and core layout (authenticated shell, navigation, role-aware sidebar).

payload.api_client: typed fetch wrapper matching OpenAPI spec

acceptance_criteria: Vite dev server starts with zero TypeScript errors

plan.description: Build the 3D simulation canvas using Three.js / React Three Fiber for physics experiments: mechanics (projectile motion, pendulum, collision), optics (ray tracing, lens simulation), and electromagnetism (field visualizations, circuit builder).

acceptance_criteria: All 11 physics experiments render at ≥ 60fps on a mid-range laptop (Chrome)
Experiment parameter controls update simulation in real time without full re-render

Simulation data (time-series measurements) exportable as CSV for lab report insertion

Physics simulations numerically consistent with known analytical results (±2%)

plan.description: Build 3D chemistry simulations using React Three Fiber + custom shaders: molecular visualizer (PubChem data), acid-base titration with pH curve, chemical reaction animator with state changes, and electrochemistry cell.

payload.data_source: PubChem REST API for molecular structure data (SDF/JSON)
payload.molecule_renderer: 3Dmol.js embedded in React, or custom Three.js sphere-stick model
payload.safety_note: All reactions virtual — no hazardous material handling warnings needed

acceptance_criteria: Molecular viewer loads and rotates any common molecule from PubChem within 3s
Titration simulation produces correct pH curve shape matching Henderson-Hasselbalch equation (±0.1 pH unit)
Chemical reaction animations show correct product states (precipitate, gas bubbles, color change)
All 10 chemistry experiments load without WebGL errors
Electrochemistry cell shows correct ion migration direction and electrode reactions
Experiment data (pH readings, measurements) captured for lab report insertion

plan.description: Build 3D biology simulations: interactive cell anatomy (organelle zoom + labels), DNA replication animation, Mendelian genetics Punnett square, virtual frog dissection (layered mesh reveal), and photosynthesis/cellular respiration cycle.

payload.cell_model: detailed 3D mesh with 15 labeled organelles, click-to-zoom
payload.dna_animation: double helix unwinding + nucleotide base pairing animation (Three.js)
payload.genetics: interactive Punnett square generator with phenotype probability display
payload.dissection: layered GLTF mesh with scalpel tool interaction — reveals organs progressively
payload.photosynthesis: animated molecule flow diagram (CO2/H2O in, O2/glucose out) with light intensity slider

acceptance_criteria: Cell anatomy model renders all 15 organelles with correct spatial relationships
Clicking an organelle triggers info panel with function description
DNA replication animation completes a full cycle in 30-60 seconds at normal speed
Punnett square correctly computes phenotype ratios for monohybrid and dihybrid crosses
Virtual dissection reveals 8 distinct organ layers in anatomically correct order
Photosynthesis cycle responds to light intensity slider with proportional output rates

plan.description: Build the lab report editor (rich text + data tables + chart insertion), collaboration workspace (shared simulation + co-editing panel + presence indicators), and curriculum alignment browser (standards search + experiment tagging).

payload.editor: TipTap 2 (ProseMirror-based) with extensions: table, image, math (KaTeX), code block
payload.chart_insertion: Chart.js charts generated from simulation data, embedded as images in report
payload.collaboration_ui: split pane: 3D simulation left, co-edit lab report right, participant presence top bar
payload.curriculum_browser: faceted search by grade level, domain, standard code; experiment cards with standards tags
payload.submission_flow: draft → submit → teacher-reviewed → graded state machine with visual status indicator

acceptance_criteria: Lab report editor supports headings, bullet lists, tables, images, and KaTeX math rendering
Chart from simulation data inserts into report within 2 clicks
Collaborative editing shows co-author cursors with name labels in real time
Curriculum search returns results in under 500ms for any keyword

Teacher annotation panel overlays comments on specific report sections

plan.description: Build teacher dashboard (class management, assignment creation, progress monitoring, lab report grading with rubric), student dashboard (experiment history, upcoming assignments, lab report status), and school admin panel (user management, usage analytics).

plan.description: Implement the multi-agent coordinator AI lab assistant using the coordinator pattern: central coordinator decomposes student questions during experiments, routes to domain specialist agents (physics, chemistry, biology), generates Socratic hints, and logs interactions to SAGE audit trail.

payload.coordinator_agent: receives student question + current experiment context → classifies domain → routes to specialist

specialist_agents.physics_agent: system prompt tuned for physics concepts, unit analysis, formula derivation hints
specialist_agents.chemistry_agent: system prompt tuned for stoichiometry, reaction mechanisms, periodic trends
specialist_agents.biology_agent: system prompt tuned for life sciences, anatomy, genetics, ecology

payload.guidance_style: Socratic — asks guiding questions rather than giving direct answers
payload.audit_trail: every hint request/response logged to SAGE audit log with trace_id
payload.hitl: flagged responses (low confidence, sensitive topics) queued for teacher review before delivery

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator correctly classifies experiment domain with ≥ 95% accuracy on test set of 50 questions
Specialist agent responses delivered via SSE within 3s of student query
Socratic hint style verified — responses contain guiding question in ≥ 80% of cases
All interactions logged to audit log with student_id, experiment_id, trace_id, timestamp
Low-confidence responses (score < 0.6) routed to teacher review queue before delivery
Coordinator handles all 3 domains without cross-domain confusion

plan.description: Set up i18n infrastructure (react-i18next + i18next-http-backend) and produce translation configs for English (base), Spanish, French, and Mandarin. Localize all UI strings, experiment labels, curriculum standard names, and lab report templates.

plan.description: Write developer documentation: API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI spec), architecture overview, solution configuration guide for virtual_lab, local dev setup guide, and contribution guide.

payload.api_doc_tool: Redoc or Swagger UI embedded in docs site

acceptance_criteria: API reference generated from OpenAPI spec covers all endpoints with example requests and responses
Architecture overview includes sequence diagram for student experiment session with AI assistant
Local dev setup guide enables a new developer to run the full stack in under 30 minutes
virtual_lab solution config guide explains all project.yaml, prompts.yaml, tasks.yaml fields
Docs site builds without broken links or missing assets

flaws: Steps 16-18 assume 26+ production-quality 3D simulations can be built as frontend tasks with no asset creation strategy. A single high-fidelity virtual frog dissection with 8 anatomically correct organ layers requires either months of 3D modeling work or expensive licensed GLTF assets. This is not a frontend coding task — it is a 3D content production problem, and the plan has no budget, vendor, or timeline for it.

COPPA 'verifiable parental consent' is a legal standard with specific FTC requirements (direct notice, explicit consent mechanisms). The plan's 'age gate → parent email → consent token' flow does not meet the 'verifiable' standard unless the verification method is one of: credit card verification, toll-free phone number, digital certificate, or video conference. A parent email alone does not satisfy this. Step 11 will fail a legal review.

Step 21 HITL model is operationally unworkable: low-confidence AI responses are 'routed to teacher review before delivery' during live experiment sessions. This means a teacher must be monitoring a review queue in real time while simultaneously teaching a class. In practice, responses will be blocked indefinitely, destroying the student experience. This design has never been used successfully in any real-time classroom AI product.

Step 13 uses 'keyword + embedding similarity match' for curriculum alignment but Step 7 (database) specifies only PostgreSQL with no vector database or pgvector extension. Embedding similarity requires a vector store. This is a missing infrastructure dependency between two steps.

Step 17 lists PubChem REST API as a runtime dependency for loading molecular structures during active student sessions. PubChem has rate limits, occasional downtime, and no SLA. A chemistry class of 30 students simultaneously loading molecules will hit rate limits. No caching layer, CDN, or local molecular data fallback is specified.

Steps 16-18 have a '≥60fps on a mid-range laptop' acceptance criterion with no definition of 'mid-range' and no WebGL fallback strategy. School-issued Chromebooks with ARM chips, older iPads, and Windows devices with Intel HD 4000 graphics routinely fail complex Three.js scenes. No mention of LOD switching, quality tiers, or a 2D fallback for low-end hardware.

Rapier.js WASM physics engine (Step 16) adds significant bundle weight and has a cold-start WASM compilation delay. On first load, 3D physics experiments will have a noticeable freeze. No preloading, service worker caching, or streaming compilation strategy is mentioned.

Step 14 describes Yjs CRDT collaboration with 'server-side Y.js via websocket relay' but does not specify CRDT state persistence. If a student disconnects and reconnects, where does the current Y.Doc state come from? Redis holds transient pub/sub but not CRDT document state. This is a data loss failure mode that will occur daily.

The AI domain classifier (Step 21) requires '≥95% accuracy on a test set of 50 questions' — 50 questions is statistically insufficient to validate 95% accuracy. Cross-domain questions (e.g., biophysics, biochemistry, molecular biology) are common in high school and will fail silently with no routing fallback.

Step 22 requires '100% translation key coverage for en/es/fr/zh-CN' with no mention of how translations are produced. Scientific terminology in Mandarin and French (e.g., 'Henderson-Hasselbalch equation,' 'Punnett square genetics') translated by machine will be inaccurate or nonsensical. No professional translator budget or review process is specified.

Step 26 requires 'COPPA under-13 E2E test verifies parent email is sent' but no email service (SES, SendGrid, Postmark) is provisioned anywhere in the infrastructure plan (Step 23). The COPPA consent flow, onboarding, teacher assignment notifications, and breach notification (72h legal requirement) all depend on email, but no email provider appears in environment variables, docker-compose, or devops setup.

Step 9 threat model covers IDOR on lab reports as a key threat but maps the mitigation to 'RBAC on all endpoints.' RBAC is role-level, not object-level authorization. A student could access another student's lab report if they know the report ID. Object-level authorization (verify report.owner_id == current_user.id) is a separate control not explicitly specified in Step 11.

Steps 16, 17, and 18 run in parallel (all depend on 15 and 12) and together represent 26+ distinct 3D simulations across three scientific domains. Running these in parallel requires at minimum 3 separate teams with 3D expertise. A single developer or agent cannot build the physics, chemistry, and biology simulation sets concurrently. No resource allocation or parallelization staffing model is acknowledged.
suggestions: Add a Step 0 or sub-task to Steps 16-18 specifically for 3D asset sourcing: evaluate Sketchfab licensing, BioDigital Human API for biology, commercial GLTF asset packs for molecular models, and contract a 3D artist for custom biology meshes. This must be resolved before any frontend 3D coding begins.

Replace the parent email consent flow with COPPA-compliant options: (1) credit card microcharge ($0.50 refunded) as verifiable method, (2) integration with a COPPA safe harbor certified service like Veritas or PRIVO. Document the chosen method explicitly in the compliance artifacts.

Redesign the AI assistant HITL model: instead of blocking delivery pending teacher review, use async review — deliver the response with a 'pending teacher verification' watermark, log it, and allow teacher correction post-delivery. This matches how Google Classroom and Khan Academy handle this problem.
Add pgvector to the PostgreSQL schema in Step 7, or add ChromaDB as a service in Step 23's docker-compose. Without this, the curriculum alignment engine in Step 13 has no vector similarity backend.

Add a PubChem data import pipeline that pre-loads the top 1,000 common curriculum molecules into your own storage at deploy time. Runtime PubChem calls should be a cache miss path only, not the primary load path.

Define three hardware tiers (high/mid/low) with explicit GPU specs. For low-end, implement 2D canvas fallbacks for all simulations — this is essential for equitable access in under-resourced schools, which are your primary K-12 market.

Add Yjs document state persistence: store the serialized Y.Doc binary in PostgreSQL (BYTEA column) on each collaboration session, updated every 10 seconds and on room close. Restore from this on reconnect. Without this, disconnection = data loss.

Add email service (SES or SendGrid) to Step 23 infrastructure as a required service. Add EMAIL_FROM and SMTP_URL to environment variables. This unblocks COPPA consent, teacher notifications, and breach notification legal obligations.

Expand the AI classifier test set to at minimum 200 questions covering cross-domain edge cases. Define the ground truth labeling methodology before writing the acceptance criterion. 50 questions cannot statistically demonstrate 95% accuracy.

Add a Parental Portal page (not just a consent email) — parents under COPPA have the right to review their child's data, request deletion, and withdraw consent at any time. This requires a dedicated authenticated portal, not just email links.
missing: 3D asset sourcing and licensing strategy — the plan assumes 3D models exist but provides no source for biology anatomy meshes, molecular structures (beyond runtime PubChem), or physics simulation visual assets
Email service infrastructure — required for COPPA consent, teacher notifications, breach notification, and password reset, but absent from all devops and infrastructure steps
Parental portal / parent account type — COPPA grants parents ongoing access rights (review, delete, withdraw consent); no parent-facing UI is designed or built
Content moderation for student-generated content — lab reports and collaboration chat among minors require profanity/harmful content filtering, especially for the under-13 user segment
CDN strategy for 3D assets, WASM binaries, and textures — these assets will be 10-100MB per simulation domain; no asset delivery or lazy-loading strategy is defined
pgvector extension or vector database — required by Step 13's curriculum alignment engine but absent from Step 7 database schema and Step 23 infrastructure
Disaster recovery and backup — no database backup schedule, point-in-time recovery, or RTO/RPO targets defined for a product holding student educational records

LTI 1.3 certification process for LMS integrations — Canvas, Schoology, and Google Classroom each require separate OAuth app approval and LTI certification; these are multi-month vendor processes, not engineering tasks

Redis HA configuration — Redis is used for session state and collaboration pub/sub but is specified as a single instance with no sentinel, cluster, or persistence config; a Redis restart loses all active sessions
Hardware tier definitions and 2D fallback strategy for low-end school hardware
Breach incident response runbook — Step 6 includes '72h breach notification' in legal docs but no operational IR procedure exists to actually detect, contain, and notify within 72 hours
API rate limiting configuration per user tier — student accounts hitting AI assistant endpoints during a class could exhaust LLM API budget; no per-user or per-class rate limiting is specified
security_risks: IDOR on lab reports: RBAC controls role-level access but does not prevent student A from reading student B's report by guessing the report UUID. Object-level ownership checks must be explicitly implemented and tested in Step 11 — the threat model names this but the mitigation is incomplete.

WebSocket JWT exposure: WebSocket upgrade requests commonly pass tokens as URL query parameters (wss://host/room/123?token=...), which are logged by load balancers, proxy servers, and browser history. Step 14 specifies 'JWT token in handshake' without requiring header-based authentication, leaving this open.

Redis session state contains student PII without an explicit encryption-at-rest requirement. Step 7 requires AES-256 for PostgreSQL PII fields but places no equivalent requirement on Redis, which holds active session state including user identity.

TipTap rich text editor (Step 19) processes student-submitted HTML/JSON that will be stored and rendered for other users (teacher grading view). Without explicit server-side sanitization of TipTap JSON before storage, stored XSS is possible through malformed extension payloads.

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is listed as an environment variable in Step 23 with no mention of secrets rotation, vault integration (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or access auditing. A compromised deployment environment exposes this key.

Age verification bypass: the age gate relies on self-reported date of birth. A minor can enter a false birthdate to bypass COPPA controls. No behavioral or verification signal supplements the self-report. This is a known, documented attack vector against COPPA implementations.

Multi-tenant Redis key collisions: collaboration rooms and session state stored in Redis must be namespaced by tenant (school district). Without explicit key prefixing strategy (e.g., {tenant_id}:{room_id}), a key collision between two districts would expose cross-tenant session state.

PubChem API response injection: molecular data loaded from PubChem at runtime and rendered via 3Dmol.js or Three.js is not mentioned as being sanitized. A compromised or spoofed PubChem response could inject malicious data into the 3D renderer.
final_review.summary: This plan is thorough in its compliance, legal, and testing coverage — the COPPA/FERPA artifacts, STRIDE threat model, WCAG accessibility audit, and multi-level test strategy are well-specified. However, it fails on two fundamental dimensions that prevent it from shipping as written. First, the 3D simulation scope (26+ experiments across Steps 16-18) is treated as a software engineering problem when it is primarily a 3D content production problem: the plan has no asset sourcing strategy, no 3D artist budget, and no fallback for the low-end school hardware that represents the majority of the K-12 addressable market. Second, the COPPA 'verifiable parental consent' implementation does not meet the FTC's legal standard, which exposes the company to regulatory action at launch. Beyond these blockers, three missing infrastructure elements (email service, vector database, Redis HA) would cause production failures within the first week of operation. The plan should be revised with a dedicated pre-build step for 3D asset strategy, a corrected COPPA consent mechanism, and explicit infrastructure additions before any implementation work begins.

flaws: Steps 16-18 assume 26+ production-quality 3D simulations can be built as frontend tasks with no asset creation strategy. A single high-fidelity virtual frog dissection with 8 anatomically correct organ layers requires either months of 3D modeling work or expensive licensed GLTF assets. This is not a frontend coding task — it is a 3D content production problem, and the plan has no budget, vendor, or timeline for it.

COPPA 'verifiable parental consent' is a legal standard with specific FTC requirements (direct notice, explicit consent mechanisms). The plan's 'age gate → parent email → consent token' flow does not meet the 'verifiable' standard unless the verification method is one of: credit card verification, toll-free phone number, digital certificate, or video conference. A parent email alone does not satisfy this. Step 11 will fail a legal review.

Step 21 HITL model is operationally unworkable: low-confidence AI responses are 'routed to teacher review before delivery' during live experiment sessions. This means a teacher must be monitoring a review queue in real time while simultaneously teaching a class. In practice, responses will be blocked indefinitely, destroying the student experience. This design has never been used successfully in any real-time classroom AI product.

Step 13 uses 'keyword + embedding similarity match' for curriculum alignment but Step 7 (database) specifies only PostgreSQL with no vector database or pgvector extension. Embedding similarity requires a vector store. This is a missing infrastructure dependency between two steps.

Step 17 lists PubChem REST API as a runtime dependency for loading molecular structures during active student sessions. PubChem has rate limits, occasional downtime, and no SLA. A chemistry class of 30 students simultaneously loading molecules will hit rate limits. No caching layer, CDN, or local molecular data fallback is specified.

Steps 16-18 have a '≥60fps on a mid-range laptop' acceptance criterion with no definition of 'mid-range' and no WebGL fallback strategy. School-issued Chromebooks with ARM chips, older iPads, and Windows devices with Intel HD 4000 graphics routinely fail complex Three.js scenes. No mention of LOD switching, quality tiers, or a 2D fallback for low-end hardware.

Rapier.js WASM physics engine (Step 16) adds significant bundle weight and has a cold-start WASM compilation delay. On first load, 3D physics experiments will have a noticeable freeze. No preloading, service worker caching, or streaming compilation strategy is mentioned.

Step 14 describes Yjs CRDT collaboration with 'server-side Y.js via websocket relay' but does not specify CRDT state persistence. If a student disconnects and reconnects, where does the current Y.Doc state come from? Redis holds transient pub/sub but not CRDT document state. This is a data loss failure mode that will occur daily.

The AI domain classifier (Step 21) requires '≥95% accuracy on a test set of 50 questions' — 50 questions is statistically insufficient to validate 95% accuracy. Cross-domain questions (e.g., biophysics, biochemistry, molecular biology) are common in high school and will fail silently with no routing fallback.

Step 22 requires '100% translation key coverage for en/es/fr/zh-CN' with no mention of how translations are produced. Scientific terminology in Mandarin and French (e.g., 'Henderson-Hasselbalch equation,' 'Punnett square genetics') translated by machine will be inaccurate or nonsensical. No professional translator budget or review process is specified.

Step 26 requires 'COPPA under-13 E2E test verifies parent email is sent' but no email service (SES, SendGrid, Postmark) is provisioned anywhere in the infrastructure plan (Step 23). The COPPA consent flow, onboarding, teacher assignment notifications, and breach notification (72h legal requirement) all depend on email, but no email provider appears in environment variables, docker-compose, or devops setup.

Step 9 threat model covers IDOR on lab reports as a key threat but maps the mitigation to 'RBAC on all endpoints.' RBAC is role-level, not object-level authorization. A student could access another student's lab report if they know the report ID. Object-level authorization (verify report.owner_id == current_user.id) is a separate control not explicitly specified in Step 11.

Steps 16, 17, and 18 run in parallel (all depend on 15 and 12) and together represent 26+ distinct 3D simulations across three scientific domains. Running these in parallel requires at minimum 3 separate teams with 3D expertise. A single developer or agent cannot build the physics, chemistry, and biology simulation sets concurrently. No resource allocation or parallelization staffing model is acknowledged.
suggestions: Add a Step 0 or sub-task to Steps 16-18 specifically for 3D asset sourcing: evaluate Sketchfab licensing, BioDigital Human API for biology, commercial GLTF asset packs for molecular models, and contract a 3D artist for custom biology meshes. This must be resolved before any frontend 3D coding begins.

Replace the parent email consent flow with COPPA-compliant options: (1) credit card microcharge ($0.50 refunded) as verifiable method, (2) integration with a COPPA safe harbor certified service like Veritas or PRIVO. Document the chosen method explicitly in the compliance artifacts.

Redesign the AI assistant HITL model: instead of blocking delivery pending teacher review, use async review — deliver the response with a 'pending teacher verification' watermark, log it, and allow teacher correction post-delivery. This matches how Google Classroom and Khan Academy handle this problem.
Add pgvector to the PostgreSQL schema in Step 7, or add ChromaDB as a service in Step 23's docker-compose. Without this, the curriculum alignment engine in Step 13 has no vector similarity backend.

Add a PubChem data import pipeline that pre-loads the top 1,000 common curriculum molecules into your own storage at deploy time. Runtime PubChem calls should be a cache miss path only, not the primary load path.

Define three hardware tiers (high/mid/low) with explicit GPU specs. For low-end, implement 2D canvas fallbacks for all simulations — this is essential for equitable access in under-resourced schools, which are your primary K-12 market.

Add Yjs document state persistence: store the serialized Y.Doc binary in PostgreSQL (BYTEA column) on each collaboration session, updated every 10 seconds and on room close. Restore from this on reconnect. Without this, disconnection = data loss.

Add email service (SES or SendGrid) to Step 23 infrastructure as a required service. Add EMAIL_FROM and SMTP_URL to environment variables. This unblocks COPPA consent, teacher notifications, and breach notification legal obligations.

Expand the AI classifier test set to at minimum 200 questions covering cross-domain edge cases. Define the ground truth labeling methodology before writing the acceptance criterion. 50 questions cannot statistically demonstrate 95% accuracy.

Add a Parental Portal page (not just a consent email) — parents under COPPA have the right to review their child's data, request deletion, and withdraw consent at any time. This requires a dedicated authenticated portal, not just email links.
missing: 3D asset sourcing and licensing strategy — the plan assumes 3D models exist but provides no source for biology anatomy meshes, molecular structures (beyond runtime PubChem), or physics simulation visual assets
Email service infrastructure — required for COPPA consent, teacher notifications, breach notification, and password reset, but absent from all devops and infrastructure steps
Parental portal / parent account type — COPPA grants parents ongoing access rights (review, delete, withdraw consent); no parent-facing UI is designed or built
Content moderation for student-generated content — lab reports and collaboration chat among minors require profanity/harmful content filtering, especially for the under-13 user segment
CDN strategy for 3D assets, WASM binaries, and textures — these assets will be 10-100MB per simulation domain; no asset delivery or lazy-loading strategy is defined
pgvector extension or vector database — required by Step 13's curriculum alignment engine but absent from Step 7 database schema and Step 23 infrastructure
Disaster recovery and backup — no database backup schedule, point-in-time recovery, or RTO/RPO targets defined for a product holding student educational records

LTI 1.3 certification process for LMS integrations — Canvas, Schoology, and Google Classroom each require separate OAuth app approval and LTI certification; these are multi-month vendor processes, not engineering tasks

Redis HA configuration — Redis is used for session state and collaboration pub/sub but is specified as a single instance with no sentinel, cluster, or persistence config; a Redis restart loses all active sessions
Hardware tier definitions and 2D fallback strategy for low-end school hardware
Breach incident response runbook — Step 6 includes '72h breach notification' in legal docs but no operational IR procedure exists to actually detect, contain, and notify within 72 hours
API rate limiting configuration per user tier — student accounts hitting AI assistant endpoints during a class could exhaust LLM API budget; no per-user or per-class rate limiting is specified
security_risks: IDOR on lab reports: RBAC controls role-level access but does not prevent student A from reading student B's report by guessing the report UUID. Object-level ownership checks must be explicitly implemented and tested in Step 11 — the threat model names this but the mitigation is incomplete.

WebSocket JWT exposure: WebSocket upgrade requests commonly pass tokens as URL query parameters (wss://host/room/123?token=...), which are logged by load balancers, proxy servers, and browser history. Step 14 specifies 'JWT token in handshake' without requiring header-based authentication, leaving this open.

Redis session state contains student PII without an explicit encryption-at-rest requirement. Step 7 requires AES-256 for PostgreSQL PII fields but places no equivalent requirement on Redis, which holds active session state including user identity.

TipTap rich text editor (Step 19) processes student-submitted HTML/JSON that will be stored and rendered for other users (teacher grading view). Without explicit server-side sanitization of TipTap JSON before storage, stored XSS is possible through malformed extension payloads.

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is listed as an environment variable in Step 23 with no mention of secrets rotation, vault integration (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or access auditing. A compromised deployment environment exposes this key.

Age verification bypass: the age gate relies on self-reported date of birth. A minor can enter a false birthdate to bypass COPPA controls. No behavioral or verification signal supplements the self-report. This is a known, documented attack vector against COPPA implementations.

Multi-tenant Redis key collisions: collaboration rooms and session state stored in Redis must be namespaced by tenant (school district). Without explicit key prefixing strategy (e.g., {tenant_id}:{room_id}), a key collision between two districts would expose cross-tenant session state.

PubChem API response injection: molecular data loaded from PubChem at runtime and rendered via 3Dmol.js or Three.js is not mentioned as being sanitized. A compromised or spoofed PubChem response could inject malicious data into the 3D renderer.
history.summary: This plan is thorough in its compliance, legal, and testing coverage — the COPPA/FERPA artifacts, STRIDE threat model, WCAG accessibility audit, and multi-level test strategy are well-specified. However, it fails on two fundamental dimensions that prevent it from shipping as written. First, the 3D simulation scope (26+ experiments across Steps 16-18) is treated as a software engineering problem when it is primarily a 3D content production problem: the plan has no asset sourcing strategy, no 3D artist budget, and no fallback for the low-end school hardware that represents the majority of the K-12 addressable market. Second, the COPPA 'verifiable parental consent' implementation does not meet the FTC's legal standard, which exposes the company to regulatory action at launch. Beyond these blockers, three missing infrastructure elements (email service, vector database, Redis HA) would cause production failures within the first week of operation. The plan should be revised with a dedicated pre-build step for 3D asset strategy, a corrected COPPA consent mechanism, and explicit infrastructure additions before any implementation work begins.
